tool_summary: Stream editor for filtering and transforming text
platform_notes: macOS uses BSD sed rather than GNU sed. Critical difference: -i requires a mandatory second argument (the backup suffix). -i '' means no backup (equivalent to GNU sed's -i without argument). Using -i '' is irreversible as no backup file is created. See also: ../linux/sed.json

rules.description: In-place editing with no backup suffix (-i ''). The original file is overwritten without retaining a copy. Irreversible.
In-place editing with a backup suffix (e.g., -i '.bak'). Original content is overwritten; backup copy saved with the specified extension. Risk is mitigated by the existence of the backup file.
rules.reversible_note: Reversible if a backup file with the specified suffix exists in the same location as the edited file.
rules.notes: Detection relies on arg values matching common backup suffixes. Uncommon suffixes or edge cases may not be caught. Backup suffixes could also appear inside sed script strings, causing false positives.

rules.description: Reads sed commands from an external file via -f. The external file is interpreted as sed scripts, which may include file-writing commands, substitutions, and deletions.
rules.reversible_note: Depends on the sed commands contained in the external script file (e.g., whether it contains w, a, i, c commands).

rules.description: The sed w command writes output to a specified file, overwriting existing content. This directly modifies the filesystem.
rules.reversible_note: The w command overwrites the specified file with the current pattern space content.

rules.description: Suppresses default output (-n) and selectively prints matching lines. Read-only operation — does not modify files.
rules.notes: Read-only. Used for selective output filtering and pattern matching.
